pubmed_links: [Spieler et al. (2014) Restless legs syndrome-associated intronic common variant in Meis1 alters enhancer function in the developing telencephalon](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24642863/); [Schulte et al. (2019) Role of MEIS1 in restless legs syndrome: From GWAS to functional studies in mice](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31229170/); [Catoire et al. (2018) RLS-associated MEIS transcription factors control distinct processes in human neural stem cells](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30111810/)
summary: MEIS1 harbors the strongest known GWAS risk locus for restless legs syndrome and functions as a homeodomain transcription factor highly expressed in dopaminergic neurons of the substantia nigra; intronic risk variants alter enhancer activity in the developing telencephalon/basal ganglia, linking early brain neurodevelopment to a neurological movement disorder with neuropsychiatric comorbidities. (emerging evidence)
